India has more than 5,000 listed companies across NSE and BSE.
Market cap compares a company across peers better than share price alone.
Operating margin shows how much revenue remains after core operating costs.
A high P/E can signal expected growth, overvaluation, or both.
Debt to equity matters most when earnings are cyclical or cash flows are weak.
Return on equity measures how efficiently shareholder capital is being used.
Sector medians make raw valuation ratios more useful.
Large caps are usually more liquid, but not automatically less risky.
Free float affects how much stock is actually available for trading.
A stock can rise while fundamentals deteriorate if expectations rise faster.
Headline sentiment often moves first, while fundamentals confirm later.
Promoter pledging can be a useful risk signal in Indian equities.
Revenue growth without margin discipline can hide operating stress.
A 52 week range is context, not a valuation framework.
Peer comparison is strongest when peers share business model and margin profile.
Consensus can be right on direction and wrong on timing.
Cash conversion is often more revealing than reported profit.
Commodity linked businesses need cycle aware interpretation.
Banks require different metrics than industrial or software companies.
A low valuation multiple can reflect risk, neglect, or opportunity.
